items.passage: The mitochondrion is a small structure inside the cell that converts food molecules into usable energy. It carries its own circular DNA, separate from the genetic material stored in the nucleus. Most plant and animal cells contain hundreds of these structures, though muscle and liver cells often hold many more. Their outer surface is smooth, while the inner membrane folds into ridges that increase surface area for chemical reactions. Researchers believe mitochondria evolved from ancient bacteria that were absorbed by early cells billions of years ago.

The ozone layer is a thin band of gas in the upper atmosphere that absorbs harmful ultraviolet radiation from the sun. It protects plants, animals, and human skin from damage caused by these high-energy rays. During the 1970s, scientists noticed that certain industrial chemicals were thinning the layer above Antarctica each spring. International agreements then phased out these substances, and the layer has slowly begun to recover. However, full restoration may still take several more decades to complete.

Depth perception is the ability of the human brain to judge how far away objects are. The eyes work together by sending slightly different images to the brain, which combines them into a single three-dimensional view. Other cues, such as the way nearby objects partly cover distant ones, also help the brain estimate distance. Babies begin to develop this skill within their first six months, often by reaching for toys placed just out of range. Without it, simple tasks like pouring a drink would be surprisingly difficult.

The Silk Road was a network of ancient pathways that connected China with Central Asia, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean. Caravans of camels carried silk, spices, paper, and precious metals across deserts and mountain passes for centuries. The route also moved ideas, religions, and technologies between very different cultures along the way. Merchants often stopped at oasis cities to rest, trade, and exchange news with travelers heading in the opposite direction. Although sea routes eventually replaced much of this overland traffic, the Silk Road profoundly shaped early global exchange.

Soil is a complex mixture of broken rock, decayed plant matter, water, and tiny living organisms. The top layer, called topsoil, is usually the most fertile because it contains the most organic material from fallen leaves. Beneath it lies subsoil, which is denser and richer in clay particles than the layer above it. Earthworms and small insects mix these layers as they tunnel, slowly improving the soil's structure over time. Healthy soil takes hundreds of years to form, but it can be lost in a single season through erosion.

Gravitational waves are ripples in space and time caused by the violent movement of very massive objects. They were first predicted by Albert Einstein in 1916, but their tiny effects on Earth made direct detection extremely difficult for many decades. In 2015, large laser instruments finally measured a signal produced when two distant black holes collided and merged. The discovery allowed astronomers to study events that no ordinary telescope can ever observe. New detectors are now being built around the world to listen for even fainter signals from deeper space.
